chunk_summary: This section focuses on feeding management strategies for infants and young children (0–24 months) with neurodevelopmental dysphagia and aspiration risk, referencing guidelines from NICE, ASHA, and RCH Melbourne.
learning_objectives: Understand the feeding challenges associated with neurodevelopmental dysphagia in infants and young children.
Identify evidence-based strategies to manage aspiration risk during feeding.
Learn about multidisciplinary approaches to feeding interventions for children with cerebral palsy and other neurodevelopmental conditions.
Familiarize with key guidelines from NICE, ASHA, and RCH Melbourne on pediatric feeding and swallowing difficulties.
key_facts: Neurodevelopmental dysphagia is common in children with cerebral palsy, affecting up to 90% of cases (NICE NG62).
Aspiration risk can lead to recurrent respiratory infections and requires careful feeding management.
Thickened liquids and modified food textures are recommended to reduce aspiration risk (ASHA).
Multidisciplinary teams, including speech-language pathologists, dietitians, and pediatricians, are essential for effective management.
The Royal Children’s Hospital emphasizes individualized feeding plans tailored to the child’s developmental and medical needs.
reference_quote: ‘Children with cerebral palsy often experience feeding and swallowing difficulties, with up to 90% affected by dysphagia.’ (NICE NG62)
document_key_topics: Neurodevelopmental dysphagia in infants and young children
Guidelines for feeding and swallowing difficulties
